---
title: "You are what doesn't surprise you: the simplest possible introduction to the Free Energy Principle"
draft: true
date: 2026-03-09
description: "..."
categories:
---

For the past six years I have been teaching the undergraduate course of Differential Psychology. The typical textbooks included in the course syllabus will tell you that differential psychology is about "why and how we are different". The *how* is answerred by discussing individual differences in personality and intelligence and the *why* mostly deals with behavioral genetics: a great part of the differences among individuals is explained by differences in genes.

But what does it really *mean* to be an individual?

We all perceive ourselves as different from other people and different from the world around us. This perception implies a point of view: I am here rather than there, I am this rather than that. I am a distinct something, which means that there must be some *boundary* between what I am and what I am not.

What is this boundary that separates me from the rest of the world? What exactly is the difference between being outside rather than inside the boundary? What does it mean to be *other* rather than *me*?

We may have the feeling that our boundaries are fixed and have always been there. But this is not true. Just observe a newborn infant.

Infants are immersed in a sea of uncertainty. Everything is new. Everything is surprising. We can observe how they are even surprised by the movement of their own hand that suddenly appears in their visual field as unpredictrably as any other external moving object. However, it so happens that every time that the infant's visual cortex registers the movement of their hand some other neural circuit controlling hand movement in the motor cortex also fires. The perception of the hand is therefore *correlated* to the motor firing pattern. The two always co-ocurr so the correlation is quickly tightened, turning an unpredictable external event into an anticipated consequence of the infant's own action. As this connection is established, **the hand loses its power to surprise**. (we can actually measure this with sentitive equipment registering skin conductance [is this true? REF needed])

What has happened is that the infant's boundary has been redrawn. Through this new correlation the hand — which was once on the far side, part of the surprising world — has been incorporated, integrated, assimilated. The self is not a fixed container into which experience passively flows. It is a process, continuously redrawn along the line between what can and cannot surprise you. You are, quite precisely, what doesn't surprise you. The boundary of the self is the boundary of the predictable.

How about something more important for the survival and well-being of the infant than their own hand. How about their mother's breast. Wouldn't it be remarkable if the appearance of this other moving object could also become less surprising? Well, it seems there is another firing pattern inside the baby's motor cortex that is associated to the appearance of the mother's breast: the one that translates to crying. A new correlation is established and the breast becomes more predictable. There is still, however, a significant degree of statistical independence, since crying does not always attract the baby's mother. This weaker correlation between crying and feeding signifies that the mother remains beyond the infant's boundary. Contrary to the case of the hand, there is a residual unpredictability of the appearance of the mother that cannot be totally eliminated, no matter what the baby tries. The mother remains other.



We perceive our selves as different individuals because we can predict our selves more than we can predict the world and others.  





Everything beyond that line remains. The mother, the hunger that arrives without warning, the sounds and movements that carry no reliable correlation with anything the infant controls — all of this remains surprising, and therefore *other*.




, and therefore the ongoing target of knowing. Not because the infant has decided to learn, but because this is what a differentiated system in a changing world necessarily does.


The only stable alternative is dissolution.

And what are these models? They are not copies of the world. A model is an internal structure that generates expectations — that says, given what I know of how things tend to go, here is what I anticipate. The model is always an approximation, always wrong in some respects and serviceable in others. Its purpose is not faithful representation but reduced surprise. The cat meows and I already have a probability distribution over where she might be — kitchen most likely, bathroom if the door was left open. I do not need to merge with the world to navigate it. I need a good enough story about how it tends to behave.
But notice what this implies about the nature of that story. It is calibrated not against truth in the abstract but against the ongoing project of not being caught off guard. A model that reduces surprise reliably is a good model, regardless of whether it accurately reflects whatever the world might be like independently of any modelling. And this raises a question I do not want to close too quickly: if all knowledge is a model built for this purpose — what is the world, independent of any model built to anticipate it? I will return to this. For now it is enough to hold the question open.
This argument — that any bounded, self-organising system necessarily acts to minimise the gap between what it expects and what it encounters — is the conceptual core of one of the most ambitious scientific frameworks of recent decades. The Free Energy Principle, developed by Karl Friston and colleagues, formalises exactly this claim mathematically, applying it not only to brains but to any system that maintains a boundary against a changing environment. I have arrived at it here from a different direction: not from neuroscience or from physics but from the inside of experience, from the one starting point none of us can honestly avoid. The destination turns out to be the same.
In the posts that follow I want to examine what this means for how we understand ourselves — not just as things that know, but as things that differ from each other in how they know. If the self is continuously redrawn along the line of what surprises us, then individual differences are not fixed properties we carry around like luggage. They are different solutions, worked out over a lifetime, to the same unavoidable problem: how to persist as a something, in a world that never stops changing.


Obviously because they have already happened at least once and you — or someone you trust — has already observed a *pattern*. 



To persist is already to know, in some minimal sense. The two are not separate activities.
But what does surprise actually mean here? It is worth being precise, because surprise is not simply encountering the unfamiliar. When you roll a dice with no expectation of any particular outcome, the result does not surprise you. You made no prediction, so no prediction was violated. Surprise, in the relevant sense, requires a prior expectation. It is what happens when a model you were relying on turns out to be wrong — when the world does something your internal picture of it did not anticipate. It is not the signal of ignorance, but the signal of failed knowledge. You have to know something to discover you are wrong.
This distinction matters because it reveals that pure ignorance — the complete absence of any model — is not a state of safety or neutrality. It is a state of maximum vulnerability. If you have built no structure of anticipation at all, every event is equally possible and equally unmanageable. The pressure to reduce that openness is not optional. It is the pressure of existence itself on any system with a boundary to maintain.
Watch an infant and you see this pressure running in its most transparent form. A newborn is surprised by almost everything — its own hand no less than any other moving object in the world. But the nervous system is not passive in this. It is already searching for correlations, already building associations between what happens and what reliably follows. The infant begins to notice that a particular internal signal — something correlated with the motor command to move — always co-occurs with the sight of its own hand. The correlation, once established and tightened, turns an unpredictable external event into an anticipated consequence of the infant's own action. The hand loses its power to surprise.
What has happened is not merely that one fact has been learned. A boundary has been redrawn. The hand — which was once on the far side, part of the surprising world — has been incorporated into the system that does the anticipating. And here the insight cuts deeper than it first appears: the self is not a fixed container into which experience flows. It is a process, continuously redrawn along the line between what can and cannot surprise you. You are, quite precisely, what doesn't surprise you. The boundary of the self is the boundary of the predictable.
Everything beyond that line remains. The mother, the hunger that arrives without warning, the sounds and movements that carry no reliable correlation with anything the infant controls — all of this remains surprising, and therefore other, and therefore the ongoing target of model-building. Not because the infant has decided to learn, but because this is what a differentiated system in a changing world necessarily does. The only stable alternative is dissolution.
And what are these models? They are not copies of the world. A model is an internal structure that generates expectations — that says, given what I know of how things tend to go, here is what I anticipate. The model is always an approximation, always wrong in some respects and serviceable in others. Its purpose is not faithful representation but reduced surprise. The cat meows and I already have a probability distribution over where she might be — kitchen most likely, bathroom if the door was left open. I do not need to merge with the world to navigate it. I need a good enough story about how it tends to behave.
But notice what this implies about the nature of that story. It is calibrated not against truth in the abstract but against the ongoing project of not being caught off guard. A model that reduces surprise reliably is a good model, regardless of whether it accurately reflects whatever the world might be like independently of any modelling. And this raises a question I do not want to close too quickly: if all knowledge is a model built for this purpose — what is the world, independent of any model built to anticipate it? I will return to this. For now it is enough to hold the question open.
This argument — that any bounded, self-organising system necessarily acts to minimise the gap between what it expects and what it encounters — is the conceptual core of one of the most ambitious scientific frameworks of recent decades. The Free Energy Principle, developed by Karl Friston and colleagues, formalises exactly this claim mathematically, applying it not only to brains but to any system that maintains a boundary against a changing environment. I have arrived at it here from a different direction: not from neuroscience or from physics but from the inside of experience, from the one starting point none of us can honestly avoid. The destination turns out to be the same.
In the posts that follow I want to examine what this means for how we understand ourselves — not just as things that know, but as things that differ from each other in how they know. If the self is continuously redrawn along the line of what surprises us, then individual differences are not fixed properties we carry around like luggage. They are different solutions, worked out over a lifetime, to the same unavoidable problem: how to persist as a something, in a world that never stops changing.

Further reading
The scientific framework running in the background is Karl Friston's Free Energy Principle. His 2010 paper "The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?" in Nature Reviews Neuroscience is the key reference. Andy Clark's Surfing Uncertainty (2016) develops the related predictive processing framework accessibly and rigorously. The philosophical tradition this argument touches includes Kant's analysis of the conditions of possible experience — particularly the Critique of Pure Reason — and, closer to the present, Carlo Rovelli's relational interpretation of quantum mechanics, which raises the question of whether physical properties exist independently of the relations between systems or only within them. For a contemporary case that consciousness rather than matter is the more defensible starting point, Bernardo Kastrup's Why Materialism Is Baloney (2014) is a direct and argumentative entry into the debate.


<!-- This is where knowledge begins. Not knowledge as a cultural achievement or a philosophical ideal, but knowledge in its most basic functional form: **the capacity of a bounded thing to not be perpetually caught off guard by the world it inhabits**. In this sense, you need to know that clouds bring rain, that fire burns and that an aligator is not a good idea for a pet. -->

<!-- 
What lies outside my boundary? What is the world out there like?

One thing we can say with certainty about the world is that it is not static. It is constantly changing. And mostly in ways that are not under our control. Things happen all the time. Events unfold with no obligation to match our expectations. In other words, a great deal of the time, we do not *know* what will happen next. And this can be a problem.

Not knowing what tomorrow will bring — what this external world is going to throw at my boundary — is another way of saying that I cannot predict the future. And the future can be dangerous. If you cannot predict it is going to rain you are a good candidate for getting wet and catching a cold. If you only *knew* that dark clouds usually come with rain you would have thrown that umbrella in your bag before leaving your house.

So, if we are bounded, differentiated things that persists in a changing world — we are still here and have not been dissolved, overwhelmed, or reorganised beyond recognition by unpredictable external events — it meens we are *doing* something about it. We must, in some sense, be *anticipating* the future. -->
